planting.note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked, or in late summer 6–8 weeks before frost; a biennial that fixes up to 150 lb N/acre in its second year.

directions: Scarify or inoculate seed with Rhizobium meliloti, then broadcast at 10–15 lb/acre and cover lightly; white sweet clover tolerates alkaline, poor soils where other clovers fail. The plant forms a taproot in year one and overwinters to bloom prolifically in year two, fixing enormous nitrogen and feeding pollinators. Terminate in year two at early bud stage before stems become woody; chop and incorporate so the high-coumarin stems have several weeks to break down before planting. Avoid using freshly mowed green residue as mulch around brassicas, as coumarin can temporarily inhibit germination.

planting.note: Direct-sow in early spring or late summer; behaves like white sweet clover but is slightly shorter and prefers drier soils.

directions: Scarify hard-coated seed before sowing to improve germination, and inoculate with Sinorhizobium meliloti for reliable nitrogen fixation in new plantings. Broadcast at 10–12 lb/acre and press lightly into a firm seedbed; yellow sweet clover is more drought-tolerant than most clovers and establishes well on thin, calcareous soils. It overwinters as a rosette and bolts to bloom in spring of the second year; terminate at early bud for maximum N contribution. Allow 3–4 weeks between incorporation and planting to let allelopathic coumarin compounds dissipate.

planting.note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost in mild-winter climates; uniquely reseeds itself by burying its own seed pods underground.

directions: Broadcast inoculated seed at 15–20 lb/acre onto a well-prepared seedbed in late summer to early fall; subterranean clover tolerates poorly drained and acidic soils better than most annual clovers. The prostrate stems root at nodes and the spent flower heads push seed capsules into the soil surface, allowing the stand to self-reseed year after year without replanting. It fixes moderate nitrogen and forms a dense mat that suppresses winter and spring weeds. Mow or graze lightly in late winter to stimulate pod development, then allow to dry before any tillage.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before frost; balansa clover is exceptionally flood- and waterlogging-tolerant, making it ideal for wet, low-lying spots.

directions: Broadcast tiny seed at 2–4 lb/acre (due to its small seed size) onto a firm, moist seedbed; balansa clover establishes quickly and tolerates prolonged waterlogging that would kill crimson or Persian clover. It hollows out a rapid canopy that suppresses winter weeds while fixing nitrogen throughout the cool season. Terminate at early bloom by mowing or rolling for the best nitrogen contribution and easiest residue breakdown. Excellent choice for poorly drained field margins or orchard middles that receive runoff.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or late summer; thrives on wet, acidic, or cold soils where red and white clover struggle.

directions: Broadcast inoculated seed at 6–8 lb/acre into a firm seedbed; alsike clover is a short-lived perennial that tolerates more acid and waterlogged conditions than red clover and requires no liming on pH 5.5–6.5 soils. It will fix nitrogen reliably for 2–3 seasons without reseeding and pairs well with timothy or orchardgrass in mixed stands. Mow to prevent flowering from exhausting the plant, or allow it to bloom and incorporate as green manure in the second year. Avoid using as sole forage for horses, as alsike can cause photosensitization in equines.

planting.note: Sow in early spring; establishes slowly but becomes a persistent, non-bloating perennial legume ideal for slopes and orchard floors.

directions: Inoculate seed with Mesorhizobium loti and broadcast at 5–8 lb/acre onto a firm, weed-free seedbed; seedling vigor is slow, so suppress weed competition aggressively in the establishment year. Once established, birdsfoot trefoil is drought-tolerant and persists on shallow, acidic, or gravelly soils where alfalfa fails. It fixes moderate nitrogen, flowers bright yellow, and does not cause bloat in livestock — a key advantage over true clovers. Mow or graze periodically to prevent it from going rank and woody but allow it to flower at least once per season to feed pollinators.

planting.note: Sow after last frost in warm soil; an exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant warm-season legume cover from Africa.

directions: Plant inoculated seed 1 in deep after soil temperatures exceed 65°F; lablab vines grow vigorously and can climb trellises or sprawl as a smothering ground cover. It fixes up to 200 lb N/acre equivalent under good conditions and tolerates low-fertility and mildly saline soils where cowpea struggles. Terminate by mowing or chopping at early pod set; raw mature pods contain toxic lectins, so the foliage should not be eaten without cooking. In frost-free zones it can be managed as a short-lived perennial ground cover.

planting.note: Sow after last frost in hot, dry climates; exceptional drought tolerance makes this the best legume cover for arid Southwest summers.

directions: Direct-sow inoculated seed 1 in deep at 20–30 lb/acre into warm, well-drained soil after all frost danger has passed; guar is native to arid Asia and thrives in sandy, alkaline soils with limited irrigation. It fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules and produces significant biomass even in drought conditions, making it invaluable in the semi-arid Southwest and high plains. Terminate by mowing at early pod formation before seed fully hardens; the dense residue builds organic matter in soils chronically short of it. Avoid waterlogged sites — standing water causes rapid root rot.

planting.note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost; produces a large, soil-penetrating taproot that winter-kills in cold climates, leaving a decomposed channel.

directions: Broadcast seed at 8–10 lb/acre or drill at 6–7 lb/acre and cover to 1/4 in depth; forage radish emerges quickly and develops a taproot up to 18 in deep that fractures compacted subsoil and scavenges nitrates before they leach. In USDA zones 5 and colder the taproot reliably winter-kills, leaving a hollow channel that improves spring drainage and air porosity. Where winters are mild, mow the rosette before it bolts in spring to avoid a weedy seed bank. The decomposing roots have a pungent sulfur smell for 1–2 weeks after freeze-kill — this is normal.

planting.note: Sow in late summer to early fall; perennial ryegrass establishes fastest of all cool-season grasses and forms dense sod for erosion control.

directions: Broadcast seed at 20–30 lb/acre and rake lightly to 1/4 in depth; perennial ryegrass germinates in 5–7 days at optimal temperatures and establishes the fastest ground cover of any cool-season grass, making it excellent for emergency erosion control or orchard middles. It can be used as a short-rotation annual cover in cold winters (zones 3–5) or managed as a persistent sod in mild climates. Terminate before planting vegetables by aggressive mowing followed by solarization or herbicide; perennial ryegrass regrows readily from crowns and requires thorough kill. Mix with red or white clover for a nitrogen-fixing living mulch under fruit trees.

planting.note: Sow 4–6 weeks before first frost in fall; combines the cold-hardiness of rye with the palatability and residue quality of wheat.

directions: Drill or broadcast seed at 60–90 lb/acre and cover to 1 in depth in fall; triticale is a wheat-rye hybrid that is nearly as cold-hardy as cereal rye but produces softer, lower-lignin residue that breaks down faster in spring. It scavenges soil nitrogen over winter and produces abundant biomass for weed suppression and organic matter. Terminate in early spring at the jointing stage (before the seed head emerges from the boot) by rolling, flail-mowing, or tillage; residue breaks down within 3–4 weeks in warm spring conditions. Pairs well with hairy vetch to combine nitrogen fixation with grass biomass.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before frost; black oat produces more biomass and stronger allelopathy than common oats and is the preferred cover in no-till systems.

directions: Broadcast seed at 40–60 lb/acre and cover to 1 in depth in fall; black oat (Avena strigosa) is widely used in Brazilian no-till systems for its exceptional allelopathic residue that suppresses weeds well into the following season. It winter-kills in zones colder than 5, creating a ready-made mulch mat without any tillage needed. In milder climates it overwinters and can be roll-crimped in spring when fully headed out to create a thick residue layer for direct transplanting through. Combine with a legume such as hairy vetch or crimson clover to add a nitrogen component to the mulch.

planting.note: Sow after last frost; velvet bean is the most aggressive warm-season smothering cover legume, capable of blanketing 100+ sq ft per plant.

directions: Plant large seeds 1–2 in deep at 40–60 lb/acre (or 12 in apart in garden rows) after the soil is consistently warm; velvet bean vines scramble aggressively over weeds, fixing up to 400 lb N/acre equivalent while out-competing even nutsedge and Bermuda grass. All plant parts carry stinging trichomes — wear long sleeves and gloves when handling. Terminate by mowing or machete-chopping at flowering, then incorporate for a massive nitrogen and organic-matter boost. The hard, shiny seeds require scarification or a 24-hour soak before planting to improve germination.

planting.note: Sow 6–10 weeks before first frost; forage turnip produces heavy leaf canopy that smothers weeds and a fleshy taproot that winterkills to release nutrients.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed at 1–2 lb/acre and cover to 1/4 in depth; forage turnip is faster-maturing and less prone to overwintering than oilseed radish, making it a reliable fall cover where a quick canopy is needed. It scavenges residual nitrogen and suppresses winter annual weeds under its broad leaves. The bulbous root winter-kills in hard frost zones and decomposes rapidly, releasing stored nutrients at planting time. In mild climates it may overwinter and bolt in spring — mow before flowering to prevent volunteer seedlings in subsequent crops.

planting.note: Sow 4–8 weeks before frost in fall, or in earliest spring; extremely drought- and cold-tolerant legume for challenging sites.

directions: Sow inoculated seed 1–2 in deep at 40–60 lb/acre; chickling vetch is one of the most drought-tolerant annual legumes, surviving on less than 10 in of annual rainfall and fixing substantial nitrogen even on marginal soils. The vining tendrils latch onto cereal companions like rye or triticale for support. Terminate at full bloom before pods set, as mature seed contains ODAP, a neurotoxin that is harmless in soil but hazardous if consumed in large quantities. Do not grow where children or livestock might graze the ripe seeds.

planting.note: Sow as early as the ground can be worked in spring; tolerates light frost and thin, dry soils where most oilseeds fail.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seed at 5–8 lb/acre and tamp firmly; camelina requires light for good germination and should not be buried more than 1/4 in deep. It matures in under 100 days and tolerates drought and cold better than any other oilseed crop, making it a fine spring or overwintered autumn oilseed in short-season gardens. Harvest when 80% of the pods are golden and dry — thresh by beating bundled stalks into a bucket, then winnow. The oil is exceptionally high in omega-3 fatty acids and has a pleasant nutty flavor for culinary use.

planting.note: Sow at or just after last frost; hemp grows very quickly and tolerates light frost at seedling stage but is damaged by hard freezes.

directions: Drill seed 3/4–1 in deep at 20–30 lb/acre for grain production (denser stands reduce branching and improve yield); hemp requires a federal or state license to grow legally in the United States. Plants are dioecious — female plants produce the seed-bearing flowers; for grain production allow plants to go naturally to seed and harvest when seeds are firm and no longer milky. Thresh and dry seed to below 10% moisture for storage; hulled hemp seed (hearts) is nutritionally rich in protein and balanced omega-3/6 fatty acids. Do not confuse grain hemp with high-THC varieties; certified grain cultivars contain less than 0.3% THC.

planting.note: Sow early in spring; safflower has a deep taproot that scavenges subsoil moisture, making it the most drought-resistant oilseed for dryland gardens.

directions: Plant seed 1 in deep at 6–10 in apart in rows 18–24 in wide; safflower needs a long growing season so early planting in cool soil is critical. The plant grows a large rosette in the vegetative phase, then bolts to form spiny, branching heads with brilliant orange or yellow florets. Harvest when the heads are dry and papery — cut and place in cloth bags to finish drying, then thresh by rubbing to release the seed. Safflower oil is prized for its high linoleic acid content and heat stability; the petals can also be used as a saffron substitute.

planting.note: Direct-sow at or after last frost; oilseed sunflower varieties have smaller, black-hulled seeds with a higher oil percentage than confection types.

directions: Plant seed 1 in deep and 8–12 in apart in rows spaced 24–30 in; oilseed varieties like 'Peredovik' or 'Rostov' are grown more densely than confection types to reduce branching and produce a single large head per plant. Harvest when the back of the head turns yellow to brown and the seeds are hard — cut heads and hang upside-down in a covered, airy space to cure. Rub seeds out over a screen, then cold-press for oil or roast and use as a whole food. Birds are the main pest; protect ripening heads with netting or paper bags.

planting.note: Sow as early as possible in spring; fiber flax must be grown densely to produce long, unbranched stems suitable for linen production.

directions: Broadcast fiber flax seed thickly at 80–120 lb/acre (roughly 1 seed per sq in in garden beds) and press 1/4 in into a firm, smooth seedbed; the dense stand forces plants to grow tall and unbranched, producing the long stem fibers needed for linen. Harvest when the lower third of the stem turns yellow — pull plants by the root rather than cutting to preserve fiber length. Ret the pulled bundles in water or on damp grass for 1–2 weeks to break down the pectin binding fibers to the stem, then dry before breaking and scutching. Fiber flax seed can also be eaten as a food flax, though oil and food varieties are typically shorter with more branching.

planting.note: Sow after last frost in warm soil; kenaf grows tall rapidly and is grown for bast fiber, animal fodder, and paper pulp.

directions: Direct-sow seed 1/2–1 in deep at 25–30 lb/acre for fiber production (narrower spacing promotes taller, less branched stems); kenaf is a warm-season annual related to okra that can reach 14 feet in a single season. For fiber, harvest at early flowering before stems become too woody — cut at ground level and ret the stalks in water for 10–20 days to separate the long bast fibers from the woody core. The core material (hurds) is useful as animal bedding or biomass fuel, while the bast fibers make durable rope, sacking, and paper. Young leaves and shoots are edible and nutritious — commonly cooked as a green in West African cuisine.
